messages.content: What do you find structurally disturbing or unpleasant in this sigil?

What feels most unpleasant is that the core split between slice and layout is good, but it keeps getting violated in language and structure. Sometimes @slice is “matched images,” sometimes it is ordered, sometimes @RelevanceFilter includes ordering, sometimes sequencing owns ordering, and sometimes mode owns ordering, so the conceptual joints are slipping.

There’s also a repeated tension between a clean universal surface model and three quite different engines being forced into one metaphor. @timelike is strips, @spacelike is neighborhoods, @tastelike is a single-attractor gradient, but the top-level language keeps trying to say they are all the same kind of torus layout, and that produces strain, especially where @Explore says the slice is laid out “as @strips” even though @spacelike explicitly is not.

The other disturbing part is that some important concepts are in the wrong layer. @RelevanceFilter contains UI-role logic like filter, attract, and order, @TimeDirection overlaps with @Sequencing and @Slice, and @Attractor is both a geometric center and a mutable taste state. It makes the design feel less like one crisp ontology and more like several partially merged ones that have not yet decided who owns what.
Okay, well, would you like to maybe propose some changes to make it smoother?

Then it sounds right.

If the point is to see, not to act, then #see-in-sigil-space is enough. The affordance is exposure, not manipulation. What this sigil gives you is externalized perception of your world’s structure, so you can encounter the sigils as things in front of you rather than contents trapped in thought.

So the remaining uncertainty is not a flaw. It is part of the meaning. You do not yet know what further affordances the sight will unlock, only that seeing comes first. That makes @Spatial feel more like a perceptual ground than a tool, and that is a coherent shape.
